title: Dandelion
subtitle: The digestive bitter in every garden
excerpt: Taraxacum officinale: leaf as a diuretic nutritive and root as a digestive bitter and liver herb. The most-accessible bitter in the western herbal tradition, and the one most people can grow without trying.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve (the early 20th-century botanical writer), A Modern Herbal, dandelion entry: the liver, biliary, and diuretic tradition, the nutritive leaf. King's American Dispensatory (Felter & Lloyd, 1898), the hepatic and bile-stimulating herb tradition. the herbal reference on Taraxaci radix cum herba (2009), evidence for digestive complaints and bile flow support.

glossaryTerms.definition: A herb that stimulates the production and flow of bile from the liver and gallbladder into the small intestine. Cholagogues are used traditionally to support fat digestion and liver function.
A herb with a bitter taste that stimulates digestive secretions (saliva, gastric acid, bile) when tasted. The bitter signal is the action; it works via the tongue's bitter receptors.

content.text: Dandelion (Taraxacum officinale, family Asteraceae) needs no introduction as a plant. It grows in lawns, roadsides, and disturbed ground across the temperate world, its characteristic toothed leaves (the French name pissenlit, now corrupted to dandelion) lying in a flat rosette until the hollow stem elongates to carry the yellow composite flower. The clock-head of feathery seeds is familiar to every child. As a medicine, the plant divides neatly by part: the leaf is used as a nutritive and mild diuretic, the root as a digestive bitter and liver herb. The whole plant is used in some traditions.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Gallstones. Dandelion root is a strong bile-stimulating herb and stimulates bile flow. If stones obstruct the bile duct, this stimulation can provoke a painful episode or colic. Do not use dandelion root preparations if you have diagnosed gallstones with obstruction.

Asteraceae allergy. Dandelion is in the daisy family. Readers with a known ragweed, daisy, or chrysanthemum allergy should approach with caution and patch-test or omit.

Diuretics and lithium. Dandelion leaf has a measurable diuretic action. Readers on diuretic medication or lithium should consult their prescriber before regular therapeutic use, as the combined diuretic effect can affect electrolyte balance and lithium levels.

content.text: Latin binomial: Taraxacum officinale (aggregate species). Family: Asteraceae. Parts used: leaf (fresh or dried), root (dried), whole plant. The leaf is harvested before flowering for the best mineral content; the root in autumn when inulin reserves peak. Identification is straightforward: the characteristic clock-head seed head, hollow flower stem, and toothed leaves with no stem are definitive. Common look-alikes (hawk-weeds, hawk-bits, catsears) do not harm but are different plants.
The root contains taraxacin (a bitter sesquiterpene lactone responsible for the
action), inulin (a prebiotic fibre, most abundant in autumn-harvested root), sterols, and phenolic acids. The root is the

: the bitterness stimulates bile secretion and flow, supporting fat digestion and liver clearing. The leaf is rich in potassium (which makes the diuretic action potassium-sparing, unlike pharmaceutical diuretics that deplete it), vitamins A and C, iron, calcium, and magnesium. The leaf is more nutritive; the root is more active for liver and digestion.

Maud Grieve (the early 20th-century botanical writer) records dandelion root as an important liver herb, used for liver and gallbladder complaints and as a digestive remedy. She notes the Welsh and French traditions of eating the young leaves as a spring tonic. The King's American Dispensatory records the Eclectic use for hepatic torpor, jaundice, and as a gentle laxative through its bile-stimulating action.
Chinese medicine uses a related species for liver, urinary, and skin conditions. Western herbal practice follows the European and Eclectic traditions.

Dandelion root is typically prepared as a simmered herbal preparation (5 g dried root simmered in 250 ml water for 10-15 minutes), drunk once or twice a day before meals to stimulate digestion. The leaf makes a pleasant infusion or can be eaten fresh as a spring salad herb. Root herbal spirit extraction (1:5 in 25% spirit) is available; the simmered preparation preserves the bitter components well and is the traditional form. Coffee substitutes made from roasted dandelion root have some of the prebiotic inulin but less of the bitter taraxacin.

Gallstones: as above, avoid the root if stones obstruct the bile duct. Asteraceae allergy: as above. Diuretics and lithium: as above. Pregnancy: culinary use (young leaves in salad) is standard and safe; therapeutic doses of the root should be discussed with a herbalist or midwife. the herbal reference notes no safety concerns identified at the recommended dose range.
